title: Warehouse Integration
tagline: Live read-only warehouse previews and dev-vs-prod diffs
summary: dbt Wizard runs read-only SQL directly against the connected warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, and others) so an SE can preview model output and sanity-check row counts or suspicious values without a full dbt run. dbt_show resolves Jinja end to end, including ref(), source(), and macros, and executes the compiled SQL on the warehouse to show what a model would actually return. diff compares local or deferred dev state against production state so a change can be validated for row-count and value drift before the PR merges.
whyDifferent: A general AI coding agent can only read the .sql file and the YAML config; it never sees a single row of data, cannot resolve a ref() chain into runnable SQL, and has no idea whether a model currently passes or what it returns. dbt Wizard executes against the live warehouse, so it answers from real rows: actual counts, actual distinct brand_name values, actual dev-vs-prod deltas. It distinguishes "the SQL parses" from "the SQL returns the right data," which file-reading and grep fundamentally cannot do.

capabilities.title: Preview without dbt run
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__warehouse issues read-only SELECTs against the live warehouse for instant row previews, row counts, and value spot-checks. No dbt build or materialization is required, so you inspect data in seconds instead of waiting on a run.

capabilities.title: Full Jinja resolution on the warehouse
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__dbt_show compiles {{ ref() }}, {{ source() }}, and custom macros into executable SQL and runs it on the warehouse, returning the exact rows a model would emit. This catches macro and ref-chain bugs that compiled-SQL inspection alone would miss.

capabilities.title: Dev vs production drift detection
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__diff compares local or deferred dev state against the production relation, surfacing row-count deltas and changed values. It turns a blind PR merge into an evidence-backed decision.

capabilities.title: Read-only safety guarantee
capabilities.detail: warehouse queries are read-only by contract, so SEs can demo live data exploration against a customer-like warehouse without any write risk to the target schemas.

capabilities.title: Deferred-state cost efficiency
capabilities.detail: diff and validation builds support --defer --state, reusing production upstreams instead of rebuilding the whole DAG, so dev validation stays cheap even on large projects.

capabilities.title: Automatic post-edit validation
capabilities.detail: after any model edit the validation subagent automatically runs SQL checks, dbt run --defer, and a prod-vs-dev comparison via diff, so warehouse-backed verification happens without you asking.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__warehouse
tools.purpose: Execute read-only SQL directly against the connected warehouse for live data previews, row counts, and value spot-checks without running dbt.

params.name: sql
params.desc: Read-only SELECT statement to run against the warehouse.

params.name: row_limit
params.desc: Optional cap on rows returned for fast previews (e.g. 100).

tools.returns: Result set as columns + rows with row count, plus the warehouse the query ran against. Read-only; rejects DML/DDL.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__dbt_show
tools.purpose: Resolve a model's full Jinja ({{ ref() }}, {{ source() }}, macros) into executable SQL and run it on the warehouse to return the rows the model would emit.

params.name: node
params.desc: Model name or selector to compile and preview (e.g. int_orders_enriched).

params.name: limit
params.desc: Optional row limit for the preview.

params.name: vars
params.desc: Optional dbt vars to inject during compilation.

params.name: inline
params.desc: Optional ad-hoc SQL/Jinja to compile and run instead of a named node.

tools.returns: Compiled SQL plus the executed result rows from the warehouse, with the resolved ref/source relation names.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__diff
tools.purpose: Compare local or deferred dev state against production state for a node to surface row-count and value drift before merging a PR.

params.name: node
params.desc: Model to compare across dev and prod (e.g. fct_orders).

params.name: state
params.desc: Path to production manifest/state for deferred comparison.

params.name: defer
params.desc: Reuse production upstreams instead of rebuilding the full DAG.

params.name: keys
params.desc: Primary key column(s) used to align dev and prod rows.

params.name: columns
params.desc: Optional subset of columns to compare for value drift.

tools.returns: Row-count delta (added/removed/changed), per-column value differences, and a breaking/non-breaking assessment. Requires dbt Cloud token or deferred state.

examplePrompts.prompt: Show me the first 50 rows of int_orders_enriched as it would actually run, with the refs resolved.
examplePrompts.intent: Preview real model output with full Jinja resolution before building.

examplePrompts.prompt: How many rows does fct_orders have in prod right now, and how many distinct order_id?
examplePrompts.intent: Live row-count and uniqueness sanity check against the warehouse.

examplePrompts.prompt: I'm seeing a brand_name of 'UNKNOWN' in the gold layer. Pull the live rows from stg_products where brand_name is null or 'UNKNOWN'.
examplePrompts.intent: Investigate a suspicious value with a live read-only query.

examplePrompts.prompt: Compare my dev build of dim_customers against production and tell me if anything broke before I open the PR.
examplePrompts.intent: Dev-vs-prod drift validation for safe PR merge.

examplePrompts.prompt: Run dbt show on int_orders_enriched after my macro change and confirm the support_ticket_count column actually populates.
examplePrompts.intent: Verify a macro/ref change produces correct data, not just valid SQL.

examplePrompts.prompt: My fct_orders change should only add rows, not change existing totals. Diff dev vs prod on order_id and net_revenue and confirm.
examplePrompts.intent: Confirm an edit is additive (non-breaking) using key and column-scoped diff.

interaction.scenario: An SE has edited int_orders_enriched to enrich it with support ticket data and wants to preview the live output, sanity-check a suspicious null, and confirm the change is additive against prod before opening a PR.
interaction.userPrompt: I just added support_ticket_count to int_orders_enriched. Preview the real output, check whether any order_id is missing a count, and confirm this only adds a column without changing existing rows before I merge.

events.body: I just added support_ticket_count to int_orders_enriched. Preview the real output, check whether any order_id is missing a count, and confirm this only adds a column without changing existing rows before I merge.

I'll resolve the model's Jinja and run it on the warehouse to see real rows, then spot-check the new column for nulls, then diff dev against prod keyed on order_id. Starting with dbt_show so refs and macros are fully resolved.
dbt_show resolved both refs and executed on the warehouse. The new support_ticket_count column populates, but ORD-1002 came back null.
A null on support_ticket_count means the join to stg_support_tickets isn't producing 0 for orders with no tickets. Let me quantify how many order_ids are affected with a live read-only query.

events.toolArgs: {"sql":"select count(*) as null_count from (select order_id, support_ticket_count from analytics_dev.silver.int_orders_enriched) where support_ticket_count is null","row_limit":1}

events.body: 3,174 orders have a null instead of 0. That's a left-join that should coalesce to 0. This is a data-correctness issue, not a syntax one, so it would never surface from reading the file. Now I'll diff dev vs prod keyed on order_id to confirm the rest of the change is additive.

events.code: {"rows_dev":48210,"rows_prod":48210,"added":0,"removed":0,"changed":0,"new_columns":["support_ticket_count"],"assessment":"non-breaking on existing columns; one added column"}

events.body: Validation subagent confirms: row count identical (48,210), zero changed/added/removed rows on order_id, customer_id, and order_total. The only structural change is the added support_ticket_count column.

Live results: the change is additive in shape, dev and prod both have 48,210 rows with zero changes to order_id, customer_id, or order_total, and the only new column is support_ticket_count. But there is a real data bug: 3,174 orders have support_ticket_count = null instead of 0, because the left join to stg_support_tickets isn't coalescing. Wrap the count in coalesce(..., 0) before you merge. Once fixed, re-run dbt_show to confirm ORD-1002 returns 0 and you're safe to open the PR.

seNotes: Lead the demo with the file-vs-warehouse contrast: a general coding agent says 'the SQL looks fine,' dbt Wizard says '3,174 rows are null in the actual warehouse.' That gap is the whole pitch.
dbt_show is not dbt compile: it resolves Jinja AND executes on the warehouse, so it catches macro/ref bugs that produce valid-but-wrong SQL. Call that out explicitly.
diff needs a dbt Cloud token or deferred state (--defer --state); have a prod manifest staged before you demo or the prod side won't resolve.
Stress that warehouse queries are read-only by contract. This is what lets you run live exploration against a customer-like warehouse with no write risk.
Emphasize deferred state for cost: diff and validation reuse prod upstreams instead of rebuilding the DAG, which matters on large Snowflake/BigQuery projects where a full rebuild is expensive.
The validation subagent runs the prod-vs-dev comparison automatically after an edit, so the safe-merge story isn't a manual step the SE has to remember.
